learning_objectives: Plan a usability test study with clearly defined goals and metrics
Distinguish between formative and summative usability testing
Recruit representative participants using screening criteria
readings: Handbook of Usability Testing by Jeffrey Rubin & Dana Chisnell, Ch. 1-3
Measuring the User Experience by Tullis & Albert, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Usability testing is the practice of evaluating a product by observing real users attempting real tasks. It is the most direct and reliable way to identify usability problems.
key_concepts: Formative testing: finding problems during design (qualitative, small sample)
Summative testing: measuring performance after design (quantitative, larger sample)
The test plan: goals, research questions, participant criteria, tasks, metrics
Participant recruiting: screener surveys, representative sampling, compensation
The 5-user rule: 5 participants find approximately 80% of usability problems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a professional usability test plan with annotations explaining each decision

review.value: Formative tests find and fix problems during design (iterate); summative tests measure how well the final design performs against benchmarks (validate)
Nielsen's finding that 5 participants uncover approximately 80% of usability problems — diminishing returns beyond 5 for qualitative formative testing

learning_objectives: Moderate a usability test session using professional facilitation techniques
Manage challenging participant behaviors without biasing results
Balance empathy for participants with objectivity in observation
readings: Rocket Surgery Made Easy by Steve Krug, Ch. 6-8
Moderating Usability Tests by Joseph Dumas & Beth Loring, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: The moderator's skill determines the quality of usability test data. A good moderator creates comfort, encourages honesty, and avoids steering participants toward desired outcomes.
key_concepts: Building rapport: pre-test conversation, consent process, setting expectations
The think-aloud protocol: coaching participants to verbalize thoughts
Non-directive probing: 'Tell me more about that' vs. 'Did you find that confusing?'
Handling silence: when to probe and when to wait
Managing challenging situations: frustrated users, talkative users, silent users
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live moderated test with a volunteer participant, followed by class analysis of the moderator's technique
setup_requirements: Prototype or live product for testing
Recording setup with screen and face capture

review.value: Questions that explore user experience without suggesting how they should feel: 'What were you expecting?' and 'Walk me through your thought process' — never 'Was that confusing?'
Participants verbalize thoughts while completing tasks — reveals mental models, expectations, and confusion in real time. Remind with 'Keep talking' not 'What are you thinking?'

class_schedule.topic: Usability Metrics: Task Success, Time, Errors
learning_objectives: Define and measure core usability metrics: effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction
Calculate task success rates, time-on-task, and error rates
Set meaningful benchmarks and compare designs quantitatively
readings: Measuring the User Experience by Tullis & Albert, Ch. 4-7
Quantifying the User Experience by Jeff Sauro, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: What gets measured gets improved. Usability metrics transform subjective impressions into objective data that stakeholders trust and act upon.
key_concepts: ISO 9241 usability definition: effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction
Task success: binary (pass/fail) vs. levels (complete, partial, fail)
Time-on-task: geometric mean for skewed distributions
Error rate: total errors, errors per task, critical vs. non-critical errors
Satisfaction: SUS, SEQ (Single Ease Question), CSAT post-task ratings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating usability metrics from raw test session data — task success rates, geometric mean time-on-task, and SUS scores with confidence intervals
setup_requirements: Sample usability test raw data set

review.value: The correct average for time-on-task data because time data is right-skewed (a few very long times). Geometric mean = exp(mean(ln(times))) — more representative than arithmetic mean.
System Usability Scale: 0-100 score. Below 50 = unacceptable, 50-70 = marginal, 68 = industry average, 80+ = good, 90+ = exceptional

learning_objectives: Design unmoderated remote usability tests using platforms like UserTesting or Maze
Write effective task instructions for unsupervised test sessions
Analyze video and click-path data from remote tests at scale
readings: Remote Research by Nate Bolt & Tony Tulathimutte, Ch. 1-4
Beyond the Usability Lab by Albert, Tullis & Tedesco, Ch. 3-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Remote unmoderated testing scales usability evaluation beyond the lab. It trades moderator control for speed, geographic reach, and larger sample sizes — when you need answers from 50 users by Friday.
key_concepts: Moderated remote vs. unmoderated remote: trade-offs
Platform selection: UserTesting, Maze, Lookback, dscout
Task writing for unmoderated tests: precise instructions, no ambiguity
Screening and quality control: identifying low-effort participants
Analyzing at scale: click heatmaps, task completion funnels, time distributions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up an unmoderated test in Maze with a Figma prototype — creating tasks, writing instructions, and reviewing pilot results

review.value: Instructions must be crystal clear with no room for interpretation — include context, a specific goal, and a clear endpoint: 'You want to send a birthday gift to your friend Alex. Find a suitable gift and complete the checkout.'
Red flags for low-effort remote participants: completion time under 30% of expected, random clicking patterns, think-aloud audio that does not match screen actions. Exclude these from analysis.

learning_objectives: Design A/B tests with valid hypotheses and appropriate metrics
Calculate sample sizes needed for statistical significance
Interpret A/B test results and avoid common statistical pitfalls
readings: Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments by Kohavi, Tang & Xu, Ch. 1-5
Lean Analytics by Alistair Croll & Benjamin Yoskovitz, Ch. on Experimentation

lecture_notes.introduction: A/B testing is how design decisions get validated at scale in production. Understanding experimental design and statistics is essential for UX professionals working in data-driven organizations.
key_concepts: Hypothesis formation: 'Changing X will improve Y by Z%'
Control and variant design: isolating the variable being tested
Sample size calculation: effect size, significance level, statistical power
Statistical significance vs. practical significance
Common pitfalls: peeking at results early, testing too many variants, novelty effects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing an A/B test for a checkout page — forming a hypothesis, calculating required sample size, and interpreting simulated results with a chi-squared test
setup_requirements: Statistical calculator or A/B test calculator (Evan Miller's)

review.value: p < 0.05 means there is less than a 5% probability the observed difference is due to chance. But a statistically significant result may not be practically significant — a 0.1% conversion improvement may not justify the engineering effort.
Checking A/B test results before reaching the required sample size inflates false positive rates. Set the sample size in advance and wait until it is reached before analyzing.

learning_objectives: Create usability reports that drive design action
Prioritize findings using severity and frequency matrices
Present findings to different stakeholder audiences effectively
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Ch. 4-6
Presenting Design Work by Donna Spencer, Ch. on Research Reports

lecture_notes.introduction: A usability test that produces a report nobody reads is wasted effort. The final step of every evaluation is translating findings into a format that compels action.
key_concepts: Finding format: observation, severity, evidence (video clip), recommendation
Severity x frequency matrix for prioritization
Video highlight reels: 2-3 minute compilations of key moments
Executive summaries: top findings, metrics, and recommended actions
Different formats for different audiences: designers need details, executives need impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming raw usability test notes into a prioritized findings report and a 3-minute video highlight reel
setup_requirements: Video editing software (iMovie, Clipchamp, or Descript)
Raw usability test recordings for editing

review.value: Plotting findings by severity (impact on user) and frequency (how many users encountered it) — high severity + high frequency = fix immediately, low severity + low frequency = backlog
A 2-3 minute compilation of the most compelling usability test moments — nothing convinces stakeholders faster than watching real users struggle with their product

questions.question: Write a usability test task scenario for testing a flight booking website. The task should be specific enough for an unmoderated test but should not reveal the navigation path.
questions.answer: Scenario: 'You and a friend are planning a weekend trip from Chicago to Austin, Texas. You want to fly out on Friday, March 15th and return on Sunday, March 17th. Find the cheapest available flight option for both of you and proceed to the passenger information step.' This works because it provides realistic context (weekend trip with a friend), specific parameters (dates, cities, 2 passengers), a clear goal (cheapest option), and a defined endpoint (passenger information step) without telling the user where to click or which UI elements to use.

rubric.key: Scenario provides realistic context and motivation
Specific enough to be completed without moderator guidance
Does not reveal navigation path or UI element names

questions.question: During a usability test, a participant asks you 'Should I click this button?' How do you respond, and why?
questions.answer: Redirect the question back to the participant without providing direction: 'What would you do if I were not here?' or 'What do you think would happen if you clicked it?' This response is correct because: (1) Answering 'yes' or 'no' biases the task performance data. (2) The participant's uncertainty itself is a finding — they are not sure what the button does, which suggests a labeling or affordance problem. (3) Redirecting lets you observe their natural decision-making process. If the participant remains truly stuck after redirection, note the failure point and offer a gentle nudge: 'Take your best guess.' Never directly instruct them.

rubric.key: Redirects question back to participant with appropriate phrasing
Explains why direct answers bias results
Recognizes the uncertainty itself as a usability finding

questions.question: Five users completed a task with the following times in seconds: 45, 52, 48, 180, 51. Calculate the arithmetic mean and explain why the geometric mean would be a better measure of central tendency for this data.
questions.answer: Arithmetic mean = (45 + 52 + 48 + 180 + 51) / 5 = 75.2 seconds. The geometric mean = exp(mean(ln(45, 52, 48, 180, 51))) = exp(mean(3.81, 3.95, 3.87, 5.19, 3.93)) = exp(4.15) = 63.4 seconds. The geometric mean is better because time-on-task data is right-skewed — the 180-second outlier (likely a user who went off-track) pulls the arithmetic mean up to 75.2, which does not represent any user's actual experience. The geometric mean (63.4) is more representative of the typical task completion time.

rubric.key: Explains right-skewed distribution and outlier effect
Correct reasoning for geometric mean being more representative

questions.question: A team runs an A/B test on a signup page. After 3 days, Variant B shows a 12% improvement in signups with p = 0.04. The required sample size was calculated as 5,000 users per variant, but only 1,200 per variant have been exposed. Should the team ship Variant B? Explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: No, the team should not ship Variant B yet. Even though p = 0.04 appears statistically significant, this is a classic peeking problem. Checking results before reaching the pre-calculated sample size (1,200 of 5,000 = only 24% of required) dramatically inflates false positive rates — what appears as a 4% false positive rate could actually be 20-30% when peeking. The team should wait until 5,000 users per variant have been exposed before analyzing results. If business urgency requires early stopping, use sequential testing methods (like Bayesian approaches or alpha spending functions) that are designed for interim analysis.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies peeking as the core problem
Explains inflated false positive rate from early peeking
Recommends waiting for full sample or sequential testing methods

major_assignments.title: Complete Usability Evaluation Study
major_assignments.description: Plan and conduct a complete usability evaluation of a live product. Include moderated testing with 5 participants, quantitative metrics, a prioritized findings report, and a video highlight reel for stakeholders.
objectives: Develop a professional usability test plan with measurable goals
Moderate 5 usability test sessions with think-aloud protocol
Calculate and report usability metrics (task success, time, SUS)
Deliver a prioritized findings report with a stakeholder highlight reel
deliverables: Usability test plan with goals, participant criteria, tasks, and metrics
Session recordings (5 moderated sessions, 30-45 minutes each)
Quantitative metrics report: task success rates, time-on-task, SUS scores
Prioritized findings report with severity ratings and recommendations
3-minute video highlight reel of key usability moments

criteria: Goals are specific and aligned with product questions
Tasks are realistic, well-written, and cover key user journeys
Participant criteria target representative users
Metrics are appropriate for the research questions
Think-aloud protocol effectively maintained
Non-directive probing used appropriately
Participant rapport established without biasing results
All 5 sessions conducted with consistent protocol
Task success rates correctly calculated and interpreted
Time-on-task reported with geometric mean
SUS scores calculated and benchmarked
Findings systematically coded and prioritized
Report is professional and actionable
Highlight reel effectively communicates key issues
Recommendations are specific and evidence-based
Severity x frequency prioritization is clear
Professional test plan with clear goals and appropriate methods
Skilled moderation across all 5 sessions with quality data
Rigorous quantitative and qualitative analysis
Compelling deliverables that would drive action in a real product team

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on usability testing methods, metrics, and experimental design

moderation_practice.description: Weekly moderation practice sessions with peer participants and facilitator feedback

usability_evaluation_study.description: Complete usability evaluation with moderated testing, metrics, and stakeholder deliverables

method_critiques.description: Critical analysis of published usability studies evaluating methodology and conclusions

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam on usability evaluation methods, metrics, and statistical concepts

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involving live participant testing are mandatory. Missing a participant session affects your team and cannot be rescheduled. Lecture absences beyond 3 affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Test plans must be submitted on time as they gate participant recruitment. Late submissions penalized 15% per day. Final report deadline is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Participant recruiting may be shared. Moderation, analysis, and reporting must be individual work. Sharing session recordings between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All participant sessions must be real. Fabricating test data or using AI to generate fake participant responses is grounds for course failure. Participant consent is mandatory.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Screen recording software with webcam (Lookback, Loom, or Zoom), spreadsheet software for metrics, video editing software for highlight reels, and a quiet space for moderated sessions.
